data

Federally Recognized Indigenous Nations

The following visualizations draw on U.S. Census Bureau data to map federally recognized Indigenous nations across the United States. This data is directly relevant to my research on Indigenous sovereignty and the ongoing struggle for federal recognition. I don’t want to give the impression that this is the totality of Indigenous land, this is only the top 50 nations by landmass. The intent was to show mapping from Digital History Methods, and to invite people to think about their own proximity to Indigenous people, who are often made to feel far away, and long ago. This map shows that there are Indigenous people dispersed all over the United States. and this is just the sample of the top 50 by sq mileage. Note the void of Ohio Tennessee, Kentucky, Pennsylvania etc. I do not know the whole story of how. But it is notable that these states have no federally recognized nations. I wonder if there is potential to advocate for recognition of groups within those states, although removal in the 19th century seems likely.

Data source: U.S. Census Bureau via the tigris R package.
